coredata.dc:title: A smart-phone based ratiometric nanoprobe for label-free detection of methyl parathion
coredata.dc:creator: Fahimi-Kashani, Nafiseh
Hormozi-Nezhad, M. Reza
coredata.dc:description:
The widespread use of pesticides in pest management has boosted the demands for developing highly sensitive probes for on-site monitoring. Herein we presented a sensitive enzyme-free ratiometric probe for determination of methyl parathion (MP), as an organophosphate pesticide using TGA-capped CdTe QDs and carbon dots (CDs). Unlike previous methods in which hydrolysis product of MP is instrumental in the response of the sensors, here, self-assembly of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) on the surface of non-modified yellow-emissive CdTe QDs facilitates the quenching of CTAB-QDs upon addition of MP while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains constant. Using a smartphone, the ratiometric probe shows visual emission color changes from orange-peach into pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue, which is highly appropriate for quantitative detection of MP based on the hue-saturation-lightness color model. A wide linear range from 0.001 μg.mL−1 to 10 μg. mL−
1 MP with a low limit of detection of 1.2 ng. mL−1 (S/N = 3) were obtained under optimized condition. Furthermore, with high sensitivity and selectivity, this platform has the capability to detect MP in rice and tap water samples with desired results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric nanoprobe
Label-free
Visual detection
Organophosphate pesticides
Smartphone
Methyl parathion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calibration and sensitivity assessment of the ratiometric probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images (96 well fluorescence microplate) of the TGA-capped CdTe QDs solution in the absence (1) and presence (2) 40 μM CTAB, (B) blue-emissive CDs (1) yellow-emissive TGA-capped CdTe QDs (2) and the mixture of CDs and YQDs in the absence (3) and presence of 8 μg.mL−1 MP (4), (C) blue-emissive the CDs (1) orange-emissive CTAB-QDs (2) and the mixture of CDs and CTAB-QDs in the absence (3) and presence of 8 μg.mL−1 MP (4) at pH 10 and 10 min incubation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
HRTEM images of of (A)TGA-capped CdTe QDs and (B) CTAB-CdTe QDs (40 μM CTAB) and (C) their corresponding UV–vis spectra.
Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images (96 well fluorescence microplate) of the probe solution at pH 10 and 10 min incubation in the presence of different concentrations of CTAB.
Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images of the probe at various pH values in the presence of 40 μM CTAB after 10 min before (A) and after (B) addition of 8 μg.mL−1 MP.

Fluorescence (A) images (96 well fluorescence microplate) of (A) the ratiometric probe (CTAB-QDs/BCDs) and (B) Yellow emissive QDs, upon addition of various concentrations of MP. All images were captured under 365 nm UV cabinet (C) fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric nanoprobe and linear relationship between the (D) fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe of ((I0-I)/I0, I = I595/I440)) (E) Color Hue (H) values and different concentrations of MP in the range of 0.001-10 μg.mL−1 with error bars (experiments were done in three replicate). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Ratiometric responses of the probe in the presence of potential relevant ions and pesticides which coexist in water and apple. The concentration of MP is μg. mL−1, and other pesticides and ions exist with a concentration of 100 μg.mL−1.
Sensing Strategy of the Ratiometric Probe.
A comparison of the proposed method with the other reported fluorescence methods for methyl parathion detection.

Turn-off fluorescence of MoS2 QDs in
Presence of alkaline hydrolysis product of MP
Turn-off fluorescence of red-emissive CTAB/MPA-capped CdTeQDs Hybrids in presence enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP
Turn-on fluorescence of dithizone-capped CdTe QDs by ligand replacement of dithizone with enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP

Ratiometric fluorescence signal based on inner filter effect (IFE) between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and ratiometric fluorescent quantum dots (RF-QDs :hybridized two differently colored CdTe QDs) due to enzymatic inhibition behavior of MP
Turn-off fluorescence of MIP-CdTe QDs in the presence of MP
Turn-on fluorescence of MIP-CuInS2 QDs and Pb(II) hybrid in the presence of enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP
Turn-on fluorescence of L-tyrosine methyl ester-capped carbon dots due to enzymatic inhibition behavior of MP
Turn-off fluorescence of methyl parathion hydrolase-capped carbon dots in the presence of enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP through inner filter
Turn-on fluorescence of Cysteamine-capped CdTe QDs through enzymatic inhibition behavior in the presence of MP
Ratiometric Fluorescence signal of Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots in the presence of Pb(II) and enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP
Turn-on fluorescence of UCNPs in the presence of enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP
Ratiometric fluorescence signal of N-CDs through enzymatic inhibition behavior of MP

Turn-off fluorescence of N-CDs in
Presence of alkaline hydrolysis product of MP
Turn-on fluorescence of BSA-capped AuNCs due to enzymatic inhibition behavior of MP
Metal enhanced fluorescence of pyranine in the presence of MP and organophosphate hydrolase enzyme

Turn-off fluorescence of MOFs in
Presence of enzymatic hydrolysis product of MP
Ratiometric fluorescence signal of orange-emissive CTAB/TGA-capped CdTeQDs Hybrids and blue-emissive CDs in presence MP
Water, rice, wheat and corn flours

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of MP in rice, wheat and corn flours and tap water samples.

head.ce:title: A smart-phone based ratiometric nanoprobe for label-free detection of methyl parathion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The widespread use of pesticides in pest management has boosted the demands for developing highly sensitive probes for on-site monitoring. Herein we presented a sensitive enzyme-free ratiometric probe for determination of methyl parathion (MP), as an organophosphate pesticide using TGA-capped CdTe QDs and carbon dots (CDs). Unlike previous methods in which hydrolysis product of MP is instrumental in the response of the sensors, here, self-assembly of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) on the surface of non-modified yellow-emissive CdTe QDs facilitates the quenching of CTAB-QDs upon addition of MP while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains constant. Using a smartphone, the ratiometric probe shows visual emission color changes from orange-peach into pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue, which is highly appropriate for quantitative detection of MP based on the hue-saturation-lightness color model. A wide linear range from 0.001 μg.mL−1 to 10 μg. mL−
1 MP with a low limit of detection of 1.2 ng. mL−1 (S/N = 3) were obtained under optimized condition. Furthermore, with high sensitivity and selectivity, this platform has the capability to detect MP in rice and tap water samples with desired results.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) have been widely used to control pest problems in crops. However, due to the high toxicity of OPs through inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity in the central nervous system, accumulation of pesticide residues in the food chain leads to acute human health and environmental concerns [1–3]. Widespread use of methyl parathion (MP), as an OPs acaricide and insecticide, has adversely affected somatic and sperm cells and raised considerable concerns, and thus it is strictly prohibited to use in farming as a result [4]. The maximum permissible levels of 0.002 μg.mL−1 MP for lifetime exposure of adults and 0.3 μg.mL-1 MP for short-term exposure of children in drinking water and 1 μg.mL−1 MP in rice are reported by the regulations of Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [5]. Accordingly, the development of a rapid and reliable method for sensitive detection of MP is of great practical value to guarantee public health.

A number of analytical techniques such as gas chromatography (GC) [6–8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9–11], electrochemical [12–15], immunoassay [16–19], fluorescence [20–23], spectrophotometry [24–26], Raman spectroscopy and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [27–29] have been commonly reported for sensitive detection of MP residues. Nevertheless, they experience some limitations in terms of expensive instrumentation, complicated and time-consuming procedures, needing trained operators. Furthermore, they are usually lacking in visual ability for on-site detection of targets.

The road to visual detection, have been paved with design of ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes. In fluorescence probes in which intensity of a single wavelength is monitored in the presence of the target, a variety of analyte-independent factors which are mainly photobleaching of the fluorophore, light scattering by the sample, excitation source fluctuations might affect the signal of the and reduce the reliability of the method [30]. A ratiometric-based fluorescent probe seems to be a proper candidate to tackle the challenges of environmental factors by calculating the ratio between two fluorescence intensity values at two different wavelengths. Utilizing either double emitters or intrinsic dual emissive fluorophores, a ratiometric platform provides an internal reference that significantly boosts the sensitivity and reliability of the method. Moreover, the wide color change of the probe improves the visual quantification of the target [31,32]. Therefore, thanks to its diminished environmental effect and easy-to-differentiate color alterations, ratiometric fluorescence detection using nanomaterials is a winning strategy for sensitive visual detection. Development of ratiometric nanoprobes for on-site visual tracking of pesticides is of great significance. Only a few numbers of ratiometric probes have been published for the determination of methyl parathion, all of which experience visualization challenges and also they require enzymes or conditions in order to hydrolyze MP to p-nitrophenol [33,34].

By contrast to previous reports in which enzymatic hydrolysis of target organophosphate pesticides is the main sensing strategy of the nanoprobes, in the present contribution, we have developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe, based on the combination thioglycolic acid (TGA)-functionalized yellow emissive cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots (YQDs) as sensing fluorophore and blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) as internal reference, capable of visual detection of methyl parathion in rice and tap water samples. Using a smart phone, the ratiometric probe demonstrates continuous visual emission color changes from orange-peach into pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue, which is suitable for on-site detection of MP based on the hue-saturation-lightness color space model. Self-assembly of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) on the surface of YQDs mediates the electron transfer (ET) between MP and CdTe QDs, and hence result in emission quenching of CdTe QDs. Therefore, we imposed a great improve in the field of label-free ratiometric sensing for determination of organophosphate pesticides whose determination is of great importance due to their adverse effects on human health.

Thioglycolic acid (TGA), cadmium chloride dihydrate (CdCl2.2H2O), Tellurium powder (Te) sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and analytical standards of pesticides including methyl parathion (MP), chlorpyrifos (CP), malathion (MT), endosulfan (ES), dimethoate (DM), pirimiphos-Methyl (PPM), anilofos (AF), edifenfos(FF), carbaryl (CB), carbofuran (CF), imidacloprid (IMP), and acetamiprid (ACP), thiametoxam (TM), thiacloprid (TP), propiconazole (PPC), tebuconazole (PPC) and permethrin (PT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), citric acid, urea, sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium chloride (KCl), Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2) and ethanol were purchased from Merck. The stock solutions of pesticides were prepared in ethanol (0.1 g·L−1). Deionized water, with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ, was used for all the preparations.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Varian Cary Eclipse photoluminescence spectrometer equipped with a Xe lamp. Absorbance spectra were collected using PerkinElmer (Lambda25) spectrophotometer. A 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette was used for recording either absorption and emission spectra. Size distributions and ζ-potential of the particles were collected using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (ZEN3600) at ambient temperature. Measurements of pH were performed with a Metrohm Instrument Model of 827 pH meter equipped with a glass electrode. The determination of PM in the rice sample was performed by the Sykam HPLC instrument equipped with a C-18 column and UV–vis detector. All images were captured using a smartphone camera (Samsung Note 8) in a 96 well fluorescence microplate.

TGA-capped CdTe QDs were synthesized in accordance with a procedure described previously [35]. Briefly, 2.9 mmol of TGA was added to 5.0 mL deionized water containing 1.2 mmol CdCl2.2H2O and using NaOH solution, the pH was adjusted at 9.0 to a final volume of 10.0 mL. Under vigorous stirring and argon atmosphere, Te powder (0.8 mmol) and NaBH4 (5.0 mmol) were added in order to 75.0 mL of DI water in a three-neck round bottom flask at 50 ℃. After 20 min, when the color of solution turned bright purple, 10.0 mL CdCl2 solution with pH-adjusted of 9.0 was added and the temperature was raised to 150 ℃. When the emission color of TGA-capped CdTe QDs turned yellow, heating was stopped and the solution was cooled in an ice bath. The synthesized yellow emissive CdTe QDs (YQDs) showed a strong photoluminescence (PL) emission peak around 575 nm with excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

Blue emissive CDs (BCDs) were hydrothermally synthesized with slight modification to the previously reported method [36]. In brief, mixture solution of 0.3 g citric acid and 0.3 g urea in 10 mL of deionized water was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h. The resulting light green solution containing CDs was diluted to (1:800) with deionized water and stored at 4℃ for future use.

The ratiometric nanoprobe was prepared by mixing CTAB-CdTe QDs and BCDs in an appropriate intensity ratio. 15.0 μL diluted solution of BCDs, 25.0 μL YQDs, and 50.0 μL CTAB 2 mM were added to 2.0 mL deionized water and thoroughly mixed. The ratio of emission intensity at 595 and 440 nm wavelengths was adjusted to 4:1. The PL spectra were recorded from 400 to 700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm with excitation and emission slits of 10.0 nm.

To detect MP, 300 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 10.0, 100 mmol.L−1) was added to as-prepared ratiometric probe in a microcentrifuge tube. Then 100 μL of stock solutions of MP in ethanol with various concentrations were added, and the PL spectra were recorded after 5 min until the fluorescence signal of the probe reaches equilibrium. Fluorescence intensity of CTAB-QDs at 595 nm and BCDs at 440 nm were used for the detection of MP.

Considering the simultaneous utilization of several pesticides with MP in rice fields, the selectivity of the ratiometric probe was evaluated in the presence of 100 μg.mL−1 of MP, CP, MT, ES, DM, PPM, AF, EF, CB, CF, ACP, IMP, TP, TM, PPC, TBC and PT. Due to possible interference of some ions, the fluorescence response of the probe was also investigated toward 100 μg.mL-1 of Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3- and PO4 3-ions.

The applicability of the proposed ratiometric probe was further explored by the determination of MP in a contaminated rice, wheat and corn flours and tap water samples. The tainted rice sample was provided by a local paddy field. According to a previous report, the extraction was performed by grinding 0.25 g of the rice sample and thoroughly mixing with 500 μL of acetonitrile, following by ultrasonic extraction for 30 min and centrifugation for 10 min at 4000 rpm [34]. Sample preparation of 0.25 g of the wheat and corn flours were also similar to rice. Subsequently, the supernatant was evaporated at room temperature, and then it was re-dissolved in 100 μL of ethanol. The extract diluted 1:10, and the analyses were carried out on rice and flour samples (in triplicate trials), the same as the above procedure for MP detection. The determination of MP in the contaminated rice sample was further performed by a standard high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. The water sample was filtered using a filter paper (11 μm) and spiked with a certain concentration of MP.

As schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 , the sensing strategy mainly relies on the CTAB-mediated fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs upon addition of MP pesticide. Yellow emissive TGA-capped CdTe QDs were directly prepared in aqueous solution and exhibit a narrow emission spectrum with a sharp peak at 575 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S1). The as-prepared aqueous solution of blue emissive CDs shows an emission peak at 440 nm as well. The DLS and Zeta potential measurements demonstrate the QDs are well-dispersed with the hydrodynamic size of 2.5 nm and negative charge of −40 mV (Fig. S2A, D). HRTEM image of TGA-capped CdTe QDs is also depicts well-dispersed QDs with the diameter of about 2.5 nm (Fig. 2A). Self-assembly of positively-charged CTAB layer on the surface of negatively-charged CdTe QDs through electrostatic interactions and formation of supramolecular vesicles and thus red-shift in emission spectra of QDs has been thoroughly discussed [37]. Here, by utilizing yellow-emissive TGA-capped CdTe QDs, as shown in Fig. 2C upon addition of 40 μM CTAB, there is a 10 nm red-shift in the absorption spectrum of TGA-capped CdTe QDs from 540 nm to 550 nm. This ion-pair interaction between oppositely charged yellow emissive TGA-capped QDs and CTAB also leads to a notable decrease and a red-shift of 25 nm in emission peak of CTAB-QDs hybrids, following by alteration of emission color from yellow to orange (Fig. 1
A). Moreover, size distribution of 20 nm, ζ-potential of +50 mV (Fig. S2B, E) and HRTEM image of CdTe QDs in the presence of CTAB (Fig. S2B) are all in agreement with the formation of a self-assembled CTAB layer on the surface of QDs. The inset of Fig. 2B clearly shows the aggregation of CTAB-QDs which result in red-shift in absorption and fluorescence spectra of QDs. Dipole-dipole interaction of QDs in CTAB-QDs assemblies results in red-shift in fluorescence spectra of QDs and their rapid fluorescence decay (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 1B, the mixture of BCDs and YQDs with green emission color, at adjusted 1:4 ratio of fluorescent intensity (BCDs: YQDs), shows no spectral changes upon the addition of MP. By contrast, a notable decrease with a slight blue-shift in the emission of CTAB-QDs assemblies at 595 nm is observed in the presence of MP, while the emission of BCDs at 440 nm remains unchanged. Changes in the emission color of the probe from peach to blue enables visual detection of MP (Fig. 1C). The mixture of orange-emissive CTAB-QDs and blue-emissive CDs in the ratio of 1:1 in fluorescence intensity results in a purple emission, and thus the maximum color tonality alteration is either “purple to red” or “purple to blue”. Compression of the range of color variation is mainly due to formation of intermediate composite colors. In order to design a wide-color-changing probe, the proximity of emission ratio to one of the probes helps to avoid intermediate colors. In case of fluorescence quenching of the probe containing red and blue fluorophores, the ratio of 4 to 1 of QDs:CDs results in a wide color tonality variation based on our previous studies [31,38]. The mixture of QDs:CDs are stable enough to be used for several months with no change in spectra which make the appropriate potential candidate in real field application. DLS and ζ-potential measurements of CTAB-QDs hybrids in the presence of 10 μg.mL−1 MP reveals that there is almost no change in size distribution of QDs, but the surface charge of the QDs turns to +20 mV (Fig. S2C,F). Moreover, there is a slight decrease in fluorescence lifetime of CTAB-QDs in presence of MP, which can be attributed to electron transfer (ET) between CTAB-CdTe QDs and MP(Fig. S4) [39]. In basic condition, anionic form of MP strengthens the electron withdrawing ability of the analyte. Reducing the surface charge, the positive charge of CTAB on the surface of CdTe QDs facilitates the energy transfer between QDs and anionic form of MP. Moreover, strong hydrophobic interaction between the alkyl chain of CTAB and aromatic ring of the analyte make MP easily accessed to the surface of CdTe QDs and quenches the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs through ET mechanism. Despite previous reports in which para-nitrophenol, as hydrolyzed product of MP, contributed in ET mechanism [34], UV–vis spectra of the probe in the presence of the target illustrate MP is not in hydrolyzed form (Fig. S3A). Consequently, it seems that the accessibility of MP toward the surface of QDs, and thus fluorescence quenching of QDs through ET mechanism is due mainly to the role which CTAB plays in lowering the charge and inducing strong hydrophobicity on the surface of CdTe QDs. On the other hand, unchanged emission of BCDs in the presence of MP is a witness for the absence of para-nitrophenol. Otherwise partial spectral overlap of BCDs emission spectrum and absorbance spectrum of para-nitrophenol, as hydrolyzed product of MP (Fig. S3B), would result in a decrease in emission intensity of BCDs through inner-filter effect (IFE) mechanism. By contrast to previous report [34] in which organophosphorus hydrolase (OPH) hydrolyzed MP and para-nitrophenol, as the hydrolysis product of MP, causes fluorescence quenching of red-emissive CTAB-QDs hybrids; herein, self-assembly of CTAB on the surface of smaller CdTe QDs (yellow-emissive QDs) leads to fluorescence quenching of the probe in presence of methyl parathion. In this regard, the effect of size of TGA-capped QDs on sensing was further studied. Fluorescence spectrum of red-emissive TGA-capped QDs (emission wavelength of 620 nm) upon addition of 40 μM CTAB shows no spectral change in presence of 8 μg.mL−1 MP (Fig. S5A). This shows though electron deficiency, steric hindrance of MP restricts fluorescence quenching of red-emissive CTAB-QDs probe; therefore, self-assembly of CTAB on the surface of larger red-emissive CTAB-QDs hybrids cannot mediate the electron transfer between MP and QDs. However, due to higher electron deficiency of para-nitrophenol as hydrolyzed product and also its less steric effect in comparison with MP, it is capable of fluorescence quenching of red-emissive CTAB-QDs hybrids (Fig. S5B). Therefore, in the proposed ratiometric probe, employing yellow-emissive CdTe QDs in presence of CTAB together with BCDs brings about a significant improvement in enzyme-free visual detection of MP directly. Blue-emissive carbon dots in the ratiometric probe, as a reference, undergo no fluorescence spectral changes and improve visual sensing of MP by changing color tonality of the probe.

The effects of different parameters, namely pH, time, and CTAB concentration on the response profile of the nanoprobe, were studied. Since the fluorescence response of CdTe QDs was deeply affected by the concentration of CTAB, the CTAB dependence of the fluorescence response of the probe was investigated over the concentration range from 1 to 200 μM. Fig. 3 illustrates the emission spectrum and the corresponding emission color of the probe in the absence of the MP upon addition of different concentrations of CTAB. A reduction and simultaneous red-shift in YQDs emission peak from 575 nm to 595 nm are observed by increasing concentration of CTAB to 20 μM, while higher concentrations of CTAB lead to an increase with slight blue-shift in emission peak of QDs and the solution turned turbid (Fig. S6A). In the meantime, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs remained constant at the concentration range of 1–40 μM CTAB; higher levels of CTAB cause quenching in BCDs fluorescence intensity tough (Fig. S6B). The reason rooted in gradual surface passivation of QDs by CTAB but in different phases [37]. In the presence of 8 μg.mL−1 MP, as depicted in Fig. S6C-D, the highest response of the probe and also the greatest emission color variation is observed at 40 μM CTAB; hence, it was selected as optimal CTAB concentration.

Due to the instability of CdTe QDs at low pH values, the influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of the probe (in absence and present of the target) was investigated between pH values 5.0 and 11.0. The maximum variation and stability of the emission color of the probe are achieved at pH 10.0 (Fig. 4 A–B). Removal of the capping agents from the surface at low pH values is the main reason for fluorescence quenching of the CdTe QDs and BCDs [40]. Fig. S7 exhibits a positive correlation between the value of pH (in the range of 5–10) and the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe; however, there is a dramatic decrease in the emission intensities of BCDs and CTAB-QDs at pH 11.0. Due to maximum fluorescence ratio, pH of 10.0 was chosen as the optimum working pH for further experiments.

The response time of the ratiometric probe toward MP shows (Fig. S8B) fluorescence intensity ratiometric values immediately reached a plateau with the addition of 8 μg.mL−1 MP within 5 min. Such rapid response was also attributed to the improved surface accessibility of MP obtained by the formation of CTAB-CdTe QDs self-assemblies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration and sensitivity assessment of the ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the sensitivity performance of the nanoprobe was investigated by observing its ratiometric fluorescence intensities and emission color change toward different concentrations of MP. As shown in Fig. 5 C, increasing the MP concentration results in gradual quenching of emission of CTAB-QDs at 595 nm, while the fluorescence intensity of BCDs at 440 nm remains almost unchanged. The change in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe ((I0-I)/I0, I = I595/I440) with the MP concentration in Fig. 5D illustrates a linear range within 0.001–10 μg. mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. A limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 ng.L−1 was obtained based on the 3σ/s calculation, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 5), and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The calculated LOD value is far less than the maximum residue levels of MP in water and rice samples, according to EPA [5]. Table 1 summarized reported fluorescence nanoprobes for detection of methyl parathion (MP) in recent years. The value of the LOD in this work is comparable with other fluorescence methods. Moreover, the direct enzyme-fee ratiometric detection of MP using a simply-prepared nanoprobe following by visual color changes tonality are the major plus points of the proposed nanoprobe. More importantly, under UV illumination, the ratiometric probe displays an emission color tonality change from peach to blue, as the MP concentration increases (Fig. 5A). CTAB-QDs as a single emitter probe shows only emission color intensity changes in the presence of MP, and thus various concentrations of the target are hardly distinct from one another visually in comparison to the proposed ratiometric probe (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, the applicability of the proposed sensor for visual tracking of MP using the smart-phone was further investigated. In the vast majority of smartphone-based approaches in which color of the probe alters based on intensity value, the RGB color model has been employed for detection of analyte [41–43]. However, in ratiometric probes, HSL (Hue, Saturation and Luminance) color model carries more significance for analyzing changes in color tonality. The hue value specifies the base color and is defined as a degree on the color wheel, where red = 0°, green = 120°, and blue = 240° [44–46]. As shown in Fig. 5E, there is a negative correlation between the hue value and MP concentration in the range from 0.1–7 μg.mL-1 (R2 = 0.99) with LOD of 0.06 μg.mL−1. The results show a significant improvement in visual tracking of MP using the proposed nanoprobe, which enables quantitative on-site detection of MP using a smartphone.

A rice sample contaminated with MP was provided by a paddy field. MP extracted from the rice sample was detected to be 4.63 μg.mL−1, which was close to 4.7 μg.mL−1, provided by an HPLC-based standard method. Table 2 demonstrates recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 98.1 % and 2.7 %, respectively. As shown in Table 2 determination of MP in wheat and corn flours and comparison of the results the values obtained by HPLC, as a standard method, show satisfactory results. Furthermore, the measurement of MP concentration in tap water samples was performed with the recovery of 101.3 %. The results confirm the feasibility and applicability of this ratiometric probe for the detection of MP in food safety assessment.

Many insects, fungus and weeds attack rice plants at different growth stages as well as in storage. Therefore, the broad-spectrum of pesticides have been used simultaneously to manage pest problems in rice production. Owing to the mixture use of pesticides to fight off potential pests, the selectivity of the ratiometric probe for the detection of MP organophosphate pesticide toward common pesticides used in the local rice fields was further studied. The investigated pesticides belong to organophosphate (CP, MT, ES, DM, PPM, AF, EF,), carbamate (CB, CF), neonicotinoid (ACP, IMP, TP, TM), triazole (PPC, TBC) and pyrethroid (PT) classes and have been authorized to use on rice at low concentrations. Moreover, the effect of some common coexisting ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2-, NO3- and PO4 3- on the probe response was also evaluated (Fig. S9). It is worth to mention that due to fluorescence quenching of QDs in the presence of heavy metal ions, masking or removal of cations such as Cd(II), Pb(II), Hg(II) and Cu(II) through complexation or precipitation is a prerequisite for the detection of MP. It can be seen in Fig. 6 that the ratiometric signal of the probe is quenched by MP with a concentration of 8 μg.mL-1, which is accompanied by remarkable fluorescence color change of the solution. Nevertheless, no apparent changes happen in the fluorescence intensity ratio and color upon the addition of other pesticides and ions. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe in the presence of 8 μg.mL-1 MP is not influenced by the coexistence of other pesticides and ions with concentration of 100 μg.mL-1. Fig. S10 shows the chemical structure of the methyl parathion and its potential. Organophosphate pesticides that have been used with MP for controlling pest problems in rice have no quenching effect on CTAB-QDs. Although the investigated pesticides have electron-deficient and aromatic functional groups, there is almost no ratiometric signal recorded in the presence of them. It seems that not only do the electron-deficiency and hydrophobic interaction of aromatic ring but also spatial structure of the target plays an important role based on which it can approach to the QDs surface and leads to fluorescence quenching of CTAB-QDs. The results imply the high selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual identification of MP over other pesticides used for rice protection.

In conclusion, an enzyme-free, CTAB-mediated dual-emission ratiometric probe has been successfully prepared for visual tracking of MP in rice. The principle of the probe is based on fluorescence quenching of CTAB-CdTe assemblies, while the blue emission of CDs remains unchanged. Therefore, generating a vivid concentration-dependent emission color variation of the peach-pink–purple-blue window. Self-assembly of initial ion-pairs between oppositely charged CdTe QDs and CTAB facilitates the electron transfer between MP and CdTe QDs through strong hydrophobic interaction between CTAB and MP and also enhances electrostatic interaction between MP and the probe by reducing the surface charge of CdTe QDs. The size of QDs plays a significant role in sensing mechanism such that the fluorescence of red-emissive CTAB-QDs only switches off upon addition of the hydrolyzed product of MP; however, fluorescence quenching of that orange-emissive CTAB-QDs is observed in the presence of MP. Additionally, MP can also be quantified by the HSL color model. The linear relationship between the H value and MP concentration enables on-site visual detection of MP by a smartphone and the results are comparable with those obtained from fluorescence spectroscopy. The accuracy of the method was verified by measuring the content of MP in rice samples using a reference HPLC method. Regarding high sensitivity and selectivity, this probe could have the potential application for rapid and on-site visual detection of MP in the real-world. In case of signal alteration of the nanoprobes for determination of pesticides other than the ones co-exist in real rice and flour samples, array technology would be a key remedy for simultaneous determination the targets.
Dr Nafiseh Fahimi-Kashani conceived the original idea and performed all experiments and analyzed the results by the guidance of Prof. M.Reza Hormozi-Nezhad as the supervisor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Nafiseh Fahimi-Kashani received her BSc (Applied Chemistry in 2011) from Khaje-Nasir University of Technology, Tehran, MSc (Analytical Chemistry in 2011) and PhD (Analytical Chemistry in 2016) at the Department of Chemistry of Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran. She completed her first postdoctoral fellowship during 2018–2019, and is currently a postdoctoral associate at the same department for the second year. Her current research interests include analytical applications of plasmonic and luminescent nanoparticles and the development of nanoparticle-based optical sensor arrays for the detection of compounds of environmental interest and biomarkers.

Prof. M. Reza Hormozi-Nezhad is a Professor of Analytical Chemistry at Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology. His research interest mainly includes sensing and biosensing based on plasmonic and luminescent nanoparticles (NPs), and developing nanostructure-based optical sensor arrays for the determination and discrimination of important chemical and biological active compounds.
